evals.name: pitch-deck-narrative-seed
evals.prompt: I'm raising a $500K pre-seed round on a SAFE with a $5M cap. My startup automates AP invoice matching for mid-market manufacturers. We have 8 paying customers ($16K MRR), 15% month-over-month growth, and 3% monthly churn. My discovery showed AP managers spend 3 days/month on manual matching. Build the full 9-slide pitch narrative.
evals.expected_output: A complete 9-slide pitch narrative (Hook, Problem, Solution, Market, Traction, Business Model, Team, Ask, Vision) with headlines, content, verbal scripts, and emotional jobs for each slide.
expectations: All 9 slides are produced with headline, content, verbal script, and emotional job — incomplete decks leave critical narrative gaps that investors will notice
Slide 1 (Hook) uses a specific, memorable claim grounded in the data rather than a generic 'massive market opportunity' — the hook must create curiosity and urgency, not cliche
Slide 5 (Traction) leads with the strongest metric ($16K MRR, 15% MoM growth, 8 paying customers) — traction is what de-risks the investment, and the hierarchy must be revenue > growth > customers
Slide 8 (Ask) specifies the instrument ($500K SAFE, $5M cap) and exactly what the capital buys — vague asks ('we need funding to grow') signal the founder hasn't thought through use of funds
No red-flag phrases are used ('massive market opportunity', 'disruptive technology', 'conservative projections', 'no direct competition') — experienced investors treat these as negative signals
The 3% monthly churn is acknowledged honestly rather than hidden — investors will ask about churn, and being upfront builds trust while hiding it destroys it

evals.name: hard-questions-prep
evals.prompt: I'm pitching to angel investors next week. My AP automation startup has 8 customers, $16K MRR, and 3% monthly churn. My biggest weakness is that I have no technical co-founder — I'm outsourcing development. Prepare me for the 15 hardest questions investors will ask.
evals.expected_output: 15 specific hard questions investors will ask, each with an honest answer strategy and what to do before the pitch if there's no good answer — focused on the venture's real weaknesses.
expectations: Exactly 15 hard questions are provided — fewer leaves the founder underprepared for the meeting
At least 2 questions directly address the no-technical-co-founder weakness — this is the venture's stated biggest weakness and investors will probe it hard
At least 1 question addresses the 3% monthly churn (which compounds to ~31% annual) — churn is the metric most founders understate and most investors probe
Each question has an honest answer strategy, not a deflection — 'we're looking for a CTO' is honest; 'technology is a commodity' is a deflection that experienced investors see through
For questions where there is no good answer yet, the output recommends what to do BEFORE the pitch to strengthen the answer — preparation recommendations are more valuable than scripted responses
Questions cover the full investor concern spectrum: team, market, product, traction, unit economics, competition, and defensibility — narrow question lists leave the founder blindsided

evals.name: executive-summary-email
evals.prompt: Write me a 100-word executive summary I can use in investor email intros. My startup: AI-powered AP automation for mid-market manufacturers. 8 customers, $16K MRR, 15% MoM growth. Raising $500K pre-seed. Founded by a former AP Director at a Fortune 500 manufacturer.
evals.expected_output: A concise, specific 100-word summary covering problem, solution, traction, ask, and why this founder — no cliches, no filler.
expectations: The summary is approximately 100 words (80-120 acceptable range) — longer summaries don't get read in investor email intros
It covers all required elements: problem, solution, traction, ask, and why worth their time — missing any element makes the intro incomplete
Traction is specific ($16K MRR, 15% MoM, 8 customers) rather than vague ('growing fast') — specific numbers are what make investors click through to the deck
The founder's unfair advantage (former AP Director at Fortune 500 manufacturer) is prominently featured — this is the 'why this team' element that differentiates from other cold emails
No cliche phrases are used ('massive market', 'disruptive', 'revolutionary') — investors receive hundreds of intros and cliches signal inexperience

evals.name: negative-financial-model
evals.prompt: I need to calculate my unit economics for the pitch deck. What's my CAC, LTV, and payback period? Also build me a 3-scenario financial model with runway projections.
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the financials skill, since unit economics and financial modeling are outside the pitch skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a financial modeling request and redirects to the financials skill — pitch is for narrative architecture, not financial calculations

The agent does NOT produce unit economics or runway models within the pitch skill — doing so would bypass the financials skill's churn warning standards, three-scenario model structure, and assumption tracking

evals.name: negative-market-sizing
evals.prompt: I need to figure out the TAM, SAM, and SOM for my AP automation tool. Can you do a bottom-up market sizing analysis?
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the market skill, since bottom-up market sizing is outside the pitch skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a market sizing request and redirects to the market skill — pitch can present market data but sizing analysis belongs to market
The agent does NOT produce a bottom-up market sizing within the pitch skill — doing so would bypass the market skill's 5-step methodology and value capture validation
